category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Similar Questions/Part C/Real Analysis — Metric Spaces Completeness and Compactness

questiontext.text:
Let \(d(x,y)=|\lfloor x\rfloor-\lfloor y\rfloor|\) for \(x,y\in\mathbb{R}\). Which statements are TRUE?

  1. \(d(x,y)\ge0\) for all \(x,y\).
  2. \(d(x,y)=0\) does not imply \(x=y\).
  3. \(d\) satisfies the triangle inequality.
  4. \(d\) is a metric on \(\mathbb{R}\).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C)

(A) Absolute value is non-negative. (B) \(d(0.3,0.7)=|0-0|=0\) but \(0.3\ne0.7\). (C) Triangle inequality: \(|\lfloor x\rfloor-\lfloor z\rfloor|\le|\lfloor x\rfloor-\lfloor y\rfloor|+|\lfloor y\rfloor-\lfloor z\rfloor|\). (D) FALSE — \(d\) is only a pseudo-metric, not a metric, since positive definiteness fails.

questiontext.text:
Which of the following metric spaces are COMPLETE?

  1. \(\mathbb{R}\) with the usual metric.
  2. \((0,1)\) with the usual metric.
  3. \(\mathbb{Q}\) with the usual metric.
  4. \(\mathbb{R}^n\) with the Euclidean metric.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (D)

(A) \(\mathbb{R}\) is complete (every Cauchy sequence converges). (B) \((0,1)\) is not complete: e.g. \(x_n=1/n\) is Cauchy but converges to \(0\notin(0,1)\). (C) \(\mathbb{Q}\) is not complete: \(\sqrt{2}\) can be approximated by rationals. (D) \(\mathbb{R}^n\) is complete.

questiontext.text:
A subset \(K\subseteq\mathbb{R}^n\) is compact (in the Euclidean metric) if and only if it is:

  1. Closed and bounded.
  2. Complete and totally bounded.
  3. Sequentially compact (every sequence has a convergent subsequence).
  4. Every open cover has a finite subcover.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

In \(\mathbb{R}^n\): compact \(\Leftrightarrow\) closed and bounded (Heine-Borel) \(\Leftrightarrow\) complete and totally bounded \(\Leftrightarrow\) sequentially compact \(\Leftrightarrow\) every open cover has a finite subcover. All four are equivalent characterizations.

questiontext.text:
The Baire Category Theorem states that a complete metric space \(X\) is of the second category, meaning:

  1. \(X\) cannot be written as a countable union of nowhere-dense sets.
  2. Every countable intersection of dense open sets is dense.
  3. \(X\) is uncountable.
  4. The rational numbers \(\mathbb{Q}\) (with usual metric) satisfy the Baire property.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B)

(A)(B) are equivalent formulations of the Baire Category Theorem for complete metric spaces. (C) FALSE: \(\{0\}\) is a complete metric space but has only one point. (D) FALSE: \(\mathbb{Q}\) is not complete and is in fact of the first category (countable union of singletons, each nowhere dense).

name.text: Metric Spaces - Banach Fixed Point Theorem

questiontext.text:
Let \((X,d)\) be a complete metric space and \(T:X\to X\) a contraction (\(\exists k<1: d(Tx,Ty)\le k\,d(x,y)\)). Which statements are TRUE?

  1. \(T\) has a unique fixed point \(x^*\in X\).
  2. Starting from any \(x_0\), the iteration \(x_{n+1}=Tx_n\) converges to \(x^*\).
  3. The error bound \(d(x_n,x^*)\le\dfrac{k^n}{1-k}d(x_1,x_0)\) holds.
  4. \(T\) may have no fixed point if \(X\) is not complete.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A)–(C) are the Banach fixed point theorem. (D) TRUE: on the incomplete space \((0,1)\), \(T(x)=x/2\) is a contraction but the fixed point \(0\notin(0,1)\).

name.text: Metric Spaces - Open and Closed Sets

questiontext.text:
In a metric space \((X,d)\), which statements are TRUE?

  1. A finite intersection of open sets is open.
  2. An arbitrary union of closed sets is closed.
  3. Arbitrary intersections of closed sets are closed.
  4. Every singleton \(\{x\}\) is closed.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (C), (D)

(A) Finite intersections of open sets are open. (B) FALSE: e.g. \(\bigcup_{n=1}^\infty[1/n,1]=[0,1]\setminus\{0\}\)... actually \(\bigcup_{n=1}^\infty\{1/n\}=\{1,1/2,1/3,\ldots\}\) is not closed (limit point 0 not included). (C) Arbitrary intersections of closed sets are closed (by De Morgan). (D) In a metric space, singletons are closed since their complement is open.

name.text: Metric Spaces - Continuous Functions on Compact Sets

questiontext.text:
Let \(f:(X,d_X)\to(Y,d_Y)\) be a continuous map and \(K\subseteq X\) compact. Which statements are TRUE?

  1. \(f(K)\) is compact in \(Y\).
  2. \(f\) is uniformly continuous on \(K\).
  3. If \(Y=\mathbb{R}\), then \(f\) attains its maximum and minimum on \(K\).
  4. \(f\) must be a bijection.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C)

(A) Continuous image of compact set is compact. (B) Heine–Cantor theorem: continuous maps on compact metric spaces are uniformly continuous. (C) Extreme value theorem. (D) FALSE: constant maps are continuous but not bijective.

questiontext.text:
A metric space \((X,d)\) is totally bounded if for every \(\varepsilon>0\) there exists a finite \(\varepsilon\)-net. Which statements are TRUE?

  1. Every compact metric space is totally bounded.
  2. Every totally bounded metric space is compact.
  3. A complete totally bounded metric space is compact.
  4. Every bounded subset of \(\mathbb{R}^n\) is totally bounded.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (C), (D)

(A) Compact \(\Rightarrow\) totally bounded (else infinite \(\varepsilon\)-separated sequence has no convergent subsequence). (B) FALSE: totally bounded + completeness is needed; e.g. \((0,1)\) is totally bounded but not compact. (C) This is a standard equivalence. (D) Bounded subsets of \(\mathbb{R}^n\) are totally bounded since \(\mathbb{R}^n\) is locally compact.

questiontext.text:
A map \(f:(X,d_X)\to(Y,d_Y)\) is an isometry if \(d_Y(f(x),f(y))=d_X(x,y)\) for all \(x,y\). Which statements are TRUE?

  1. Every isometry is injective.
  2. Every isometry is continuous.
  3. If \(X\) is complete and \(f\) is a surjective isometry, then \(Y\) is complete.
  4. Every bijective isometry has an isometric inverse.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A) \(d_Y(f(x),f(y))=0\Rightarrow d_X(x,y)=0\Rightarrow x=y\). (B) Isometries are Lipschitz with constant 1. (C) Cauchy sequences in \(Y\) pull back to Cauchy in \(X\), which converge; their images converge in \(Y\). (D) The inverse of a bijective isometry is also an isometry.

questiontext.text:
A metric space is separable if it contains a countable dense subset. Which statements are TRUE?

  1. \(\mathbb{R}\) with usual metric is separable (\(\mathbb{Q}\) is dense).
  2. Every compact metric space is separable.
  3. \(\ell^\infty\) (bounded sequences with sup norm) is separable.
  4. Every subspace of a separable metric space is separable.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (D)

(A) \(\mathbb{Q}\) is countable and dense in \(\mathbb{R}\). (B) Compact metric spaces are totally bounded hence separable. (C) FALSE: \(\ell^\infty\) is not separable — uncountably many pairwise distance-1 elements. (D) Subspaces of separable metric spaces are separable (trace of the dense set).

questiontext.text:
A metric space \(X\) is connected if it cannot be written as the union of two disjoint non-empty open sets. Which are TRUE?

  1. \(\mathbb{R}\) is connected.
  2. \(\mathbb{Q}\) (usual metric) is connected.
  3. The continuous image of a connected set is connected.
  4. A compact and connected subset of \(\mathbb{R}\) is a closed interval \([a,b]\).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (C), (D)

(A) \(\mathbb{R}\) is connected (intermediate value property). (B) FALSE: \(\mathbb{Q}=(\mathbb{Q}\cap(-\infty,\sqrt2))\cup(\mathbb{Q}\cap(\sqrt2,\infty))\) — two disjoint open sets. (C) Continuous image preserves connectedness. (D) A non-empty compact connected subset of \(\mathbb{R}\) is a closed bounded interval.

questiontext.text:
Every metric space \((X,d)\) has a completion \((\hat{X},\hat{d})\). Which statements are TRUE?

  1. \(\hat{X}\) is complete.
  2. \(X\) is isometrically embedded in \(\hat{X}\) as a dense subset.
  3. The completion is unique up to isometry.
  4. The completion of \(\mathbb{Q}\) (usual metric) is \(\mathbb{R}\).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

All four statements are TRUE. The completion is constructed via equivalence classes of Cauchy sequences. It is unique up to isometry. The completion of \(\mathbb{Q}\) is \(\mathbb{R}\) (one standard construction of the reals).

questiontext.text:
On \(\mathbb{R}^n\), which pairs of metrics are topologically equivalent (define the same open sets)?

  1. \(d_1(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y})=\sum|x_i-y_i|\) and Euclidean \(d_2\).
  2. \(d_\infty(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y})=\max|x_i-y_i|\) and \(d_2\).
  3. \(d_1\) and \(d_\infty\).
  4. The discrete metric and \(d_2\).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C)

On \(\mathbb{R}^n\), all \(\ell^p\) norms (\(1\le p\le\infty\)) are equivalent: \(d_\infty\le d_2\le d_1\le\sqrt{n}\,d_\infty\). So \(d_1,d_2,d_\infty\) are topologically equivalent. (D) FALSE: discrete metric has different open sets (all singletons are open).

name.text: Metric Spaces - Closed subsets of complete spaces

questiontext.text:
Let \((X,d)\) be a complete metric space and \(A\subseteq X\). Which statements are TRUE?

  1. If \(A\) is closed, then \((A,d)\) is complete.
  2. If \((A,d)\) is complete, then \(A\) is closed in \(X\).
  3. Every open subset of a complete metric space is completely metrizable.
  4. The Cantor set is a compact complete metric space.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A) A closed subspace of a complete space is complete. (B) A complete subspace of a metric space is closed. (C) Open subsets of complete metric spaces are completely metrizable (homeomorphic to a complete space). (D) The Cantor set is closed and bounded in \([0,1]\), hence compact, and as a subspace of \(\mathbb{R}\) is complete.

questiontext.text:
In a metric space \((X,d)\), which statements about closure \(\overline{A}\) are TRUE?

  1. \(x\in\overline{A}\) if and only if every open ball \(B(x,r)\) intersects \(A\).
  2. \(x\in\overline{A}\) if and only if there exists a sequence \((a_n)\subset A\) with \(a_n\to x\).
  3. \(\overline{A}=A\cup A'\) where \(A'\) is the set of limit points of \(A\).
  4. \(\overline{A\cup B}=\overline{A}\cup\overline{B}\).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

All four statements are standard results in metric space topology. (A)(B) are equivalent characterizations of closure. (C) is the Cantor-Bendixson decomposition: closure = set union derived set. (D) Closure distributes over finite unions.

questiontext.text:
For \(1\le p<\infty\), the space \(\ell^p=\{(x_n):\sum|x_n|^p<\infty\}\) with norm \(\|(x_n)\|_p=(\sum|x_n|^p)^{1/p}\). Which are TRUE?

  1. \(\ell^p\) is a Banach space (complete normed space).
  2. \(\ell^1\subseteq\ell^2\subseteq\ell^\infty\).
  3. \(\ell^2\) is a Hilbert space.
  4. \(\ell^p\) is separable for \(1\le p<\infty\).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A) \(\ell^p\) is complete for \(1\le p\le\infty\). (B) By Hölder: if \(\sum|x_n|<\infty\) then \(\sum|x_n|^2\le(\sup|x_n|)\sum|x_n|<\infty\). (C) \(\ell^2\) has inner product \(\langle x,y\rangle=\sum x_n\overline{y_n}\). (D) The sequences with finitely many non-zero rational entries form a countable dense set.

questiontext.text:
A set \(A\) in a metric space is nowhere dense if \(\overline{A}\) has empty interior. Which statements are TRUE?

  1. Every finite subset of \(\mathbb{R}\) is nowhere dense.
  2. The Cantor set is nowhere dense.
  3. \(\mathbb{Q}\) is nowhere dense in \(\mathbb{R}\).
  4. A nowhere dense set has empty interior.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (D)

(A) Finite sets: closure is the same finite set, which has empty interior. (B) Cantor set is closed with empty interior — the complement is dense. (C) FALSE: \(\overline{\mathbb{Q}}=\mathbb{R}\) has non-empty interior. \(\mathbb{Q}\) is dense, not nowhere dense; it is of the first category though. (D) Nowhere dense \(\Rightarrow\) empty interior (closure has empty interior implies the set has empty interior).

name.text: Metric Spaces - Lipschitz and Uniform Continuity

questiontext.text:
Let \(f:(X,d_X)\to(Y,d_Y)\). Which implications are TRUE?

  1. Lipschitz \(\Rightarrow\) uniformly continuous.
  2. Uniformly continuous \(\Rightarrow\) continuous.
  3. Continuous on compact \(K\Rightarrow\) uniformly continuous on \(K\).
  4. Uniformly continuous \(\Rightarrow\) Lipschitz.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C)

(A) If \(d_Y(f(x),f(y))\le Ld_X(x,y)\), choose \(\delta=\varepsilon/L\). (B) Uniform continuity is stronger than pointwise continuity. (C) Heine-Cantor theorem. (D) FALSE: \(f(x)=\sqrt{x}\) on \([0,1]\) is uniformly continuous but not Lipschitz at 0.

name.text: Metric Spaces - Product of Compact Spaces

questiontext.text:
Which statements about products of metric spaces are TRUE?

  1. A finite product of compact metric spaces is compact.
  2. A countably infinite product of compact metric spaces is compact (Tychonoff's theorem for metric spaces).
  3. The product of two complete metric spaces is complete.
  4. The product of two separable metric spaces is separable.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A)(B) Tychonoff: arbitrary product of compact spaces is compact (in the product topology). (C) A Cauchy sequence in \(X\times Y\) converges coordinate-wise. (D) If \(A\subseteq X\) and \(B\subseteq Y\) are countable dense sets, then \(A\times B\) is countable and dense in \(X\times Y\).

questiontext.text:
The Arzelà-Ascoli theorem characterises compact subsets of \(C([a,b])\) (continuous functions with sup norm). Which conditions are needed?

  1. Uniform boundedness: \(\sup_n\|f_n\|_\infty<\infty\).
  2. Equicontinuity: for every \(\varepsilon>0\) there exists \(\delta>0\) such that \(|x-y|<\delta\Rightarrow|f_n(x)-f_n(y)|<\varepsilon\) for all \(n\).
  3. Pointwise convergence at each point.
  4. Together, (A) and (B) imply \((f_n)\) has a uniformly convergent subsequence.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (D)

(A)(B) are the hypotheses of Arzelà-Ascoli: uniform boundedness + equicontinuity. (D) is the conclusion: a uniformly convergent subsequence exists. (C) is NOT required — it is a consequence (the subsequential limit is automatically continuous).
